attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a two-hour history of sudden onset central, crushing chest pain. She has a medical history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and chronic kidney disease. Her regular medications include metformin, perindopril, and atorvastatin. She is a smoker.

On examination, she appears uncomfortable. Her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 150/90 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Her ECG shows ST depression in leads II, III and avF. Her troponin is elevated. Serum creatinine is 150 μmol/L (45-90). She is being considered for coronary angiogram.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate measure to reduce the risk of contrast-induced nephropathy in this patient?
options: Perform CT coronary angiography instead of invasive coronary angiogram
Administer furosemide to augment urine output
attributes.explanation:
This patient is at high risk for contrast-induced nephropathy (CIN) due to her pre-existing chronic kidney disease and diabetes. When considering a contrast-enhanced procedure like coronary angiography in such patients, pre and post-hydration with intravenous fluids is important.

Administering intravenous normal saline is the most appropriate measure to reduce the risk of CIN in this patient. Adequate hydration before and after contrast administration is the most effective and well-established preventative strategy. It helps maintain renal perfusion and promotes contrast excretion.

Withholding perindopril, an ACE inhibitor, is not routinely recommended for preventing CIN. While ACE inhibitors can affect renal function, their temporary discontinuation has not been consistently shown to reduce CIN risk. Moreover, abruptly stopping antihypertensive medication would likely worsen blood pressure.

Performing CT coronary angiography instead of invasive coronary angiogram is not the best answer. CTCA generally uses less contrast than what is required for invasive coronary angiogram, however both still require contrast. Moreover, this patient is high risk for disease affecting the coronary arteries, hence, an invasive coronary angiogram is the gold standard investigation and a CTCA is not appropriate.

Administering furosemide to augment urine output is not recommended for CIN prevention. Forced diuresis without adequate hydration can actually increase the risk of CIN by causing volume depletion and increasing contrast concentration in the tubules.

Withholding metformin is sometimes recommended in patients receiving iodinated contrast, but this is primarily to prevent lactic acidosis in case CIN occurs, rather than to prevent CIN itself. Intravenous fluids is the most important consideration.

In addition to intravenous hydration, other measures to consider include using the minimum necessary dose of contrast, using iso- or low-osmolar contrast agents, and avoiding nephrotoxic medications where possible. Post-procedure monitoring of renal function is also important.
learningPoints: Contrast-induced nephropathy is a common complication in patients with pre-existing renal impairment undergoing contrast-enhanced imaging studies.
Pre and post hydration with intravenous normal saline is the most effective preventive measure for contrast-induced nephropathy in high-risk patients.
references: Modi K, Padala SA, Gupta M. Contrast-Induced Nephropathy. In: StatPearls [online]. 2023. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448066/
Mohammed NM, Mahfouz A, Achkar K, Rafie IM, Hajar R. Contrast-induced Nephropathy. Heart Views. 2013 Jul;14(3):106-16. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3969626/
